title: Nassau County, New York

infobox.value: [2nd](/wiki/New_York%27s_2nd_congressional_district "New York's 2nd congressional district"), [3rd](/wiki/New_York%27s_3rd_congressional_district "New York's 3rd congressional district"), [4th](/wiki/New_York%27s_4th_congressional_district "New York's 4th congressional district")

| * [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") * [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens")  * Nassau County * [Suffolk County](/wiki/Suffolk_County,_New_York "Suffolk County, New York")  * [Municipalities](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_on_Long_Island "List of municipalities on Long Island")  * [North Shore](/wiki/North_Shore_(Long_Island) "North Shore (Long Island)") * [South Shore](/wiki/South_Shore_(Long_Island) "South Shore (Long Island)")  * [North Fork](/wiki/North_Fork,_Suffolk_County,_New_York "North Fork, Suffolk County, New York") * [South Fork](/wiki/South_Fork,_Suffolk_County,_New_York "South Fork, Suffolk County, New York")  * [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound") * [Barrier islands](/wiki/Outer_barrier "Outer barrier")

([/ˈnæsɔː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*NASS-aw*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [suburban](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") located on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), immediately to the east of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), bordering the [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound") on the north and the open [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to the south. As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Nassau County's population was 1,395,774, making it the sixth-most populous county in the State of New York and 29th in the nation,[[1]](#cite_note-QuickFactsNassauCountyLongIsland-1) reflecting an increase of 56,242 (+4.2%) from the 1,339,532 residents enumerated at the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census").[[2]](#cite_note-QF-2) Its [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") is [Mineola](/wiki/Mineola,_New_York "Mineola, New York"), while the county's largest and most populous town is [Hempstead](/wiki/Hempstead,_New_York "Hempstead, New York").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-GR6-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

borders New York City's [borough](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City") of [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens") to its west, and Long Island's [Suffolk County](/wiki/Suffolk_County,_New_York "Suffolk County, New York") to its east. It is the most densely populated and second-most populous county in the [State of New York](/wiki/State_of_New_York "State of New York") outside of New York City, with which it maintains extensive [rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") and highway connectivity, and is considered one of the central counties within the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area").

**Nassau County** ([/ˈnæsɔː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*NASS-aw*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [suburban](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") located on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), immediately to the east of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), bordering the [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound") on the north and the open [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to the south. As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Nassau County's population was 1,395,774, making it the sixth-most populous county in the State of New York and 29th in the nation,[[1]](#cite_note-QuickFactsNassauCountyLongIsland-1) reflecting an increase of 56,242 (+4.2%) from the 1,339,532 residents enumerated at the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census").[[2]](#cite_note-QF-2) Its [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") is [Mineola](/wiki/Mineola,_New_York "Mineola, New York"), while the county's largest and most populous town is [Hempstead](/wiki/Hempstead,_New_York "Hempstead, New York").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-GR6-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Situated on western Long Island, the **County of Nassau** borders New York City's [borough](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City") of [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens") to its west, and Long Island's [Suffolk County](/wiki/Suffolk_County,_New_York "Suffolk County, New York") to its east. It is the most densely populated and second-most populous county in the [State of New York](/wiki/State_of_New_York "State of New York") outside of New York City, with which it maintains extensive [rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") and highway connectivity, and is considered one of the central counties within the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area").

Nassau County comprises two [cities](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York_(state)#City "Administrative divisions of New York (state)"), three [towns](/wiki/Political_subdivisions_of_New_York_State#Town "Political subdivisions of New York State"), 64 incorporated [villages](/wiki/Political_subdivisions_of_New_York_State#Village "Political subdivisions of New York State"), and more than 60 unincorporated [hamlets](/wiki/Hamlet_(place) "Hamlet (place)"). Nassau County has a designated [police department](/wiki/Nassau_County_Police_Department "Nassau County Police Department"),[[6]](#cite_note-6) [fire commission](/wiki/Fire_department "Fire department"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) and elected executive and legislative bodies.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Main_Street%2C_Port_Washington%2C_Long_island%2C_New_York%2C_Looking_East.jpg/250px-Main_Street%2C_Port_Washington%2C_Long_island%2C_New_York%2C_Looking_East.jpg)](/wiki/File:Main_Street,_Port_Washington,_Long_island,_New_York,_Looking_East.jpg)

Main Street, [Port Washington](/wiki/Port_Washington,_New_York "Port Washington, New York")

A 2012 *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* article based on the [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") reported Nassau County as the most expensive county and one of the highest income counties in the U.S., and the most affluent in New York state, with four of the nation's top ten towns by median income located in the county.[[9]](#cite_note-Forbes-2012-ranks-Nassau-12th-9) As of 2025, the median home price overall in Nassau County is nearly US$900,000, while the [Gold Coast](/wiki/North_Shore_(Long_Island) "North Shore (Long Island)") of Nassau County features some of the world's most expensive real estate. Nassau has also been ranked as one of the safest counties in the United States.[[10]](#cite_note-NassauSafestCounty-10)

Nassau County high school students often feature prominently as winners of the [International Science and Engineering Fair](/wiki/International_Science_and_Engineering_Fair "International Science and Engineering Fair") and similar [STEM](/wiki/Science,_technology,_engineering,_and_mathematics "Science, technology, engineering, and mathematics")-based academic awards as well as top [U.S. schools](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Education "U.S. Department of Education") lists.[[11]](#cite_note-Society_for_Science_&_the_Public-11) [Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory](/wiki/Cold_Spring_Harbor_Laboratory "Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory") in the [Town of Oyster Bay](/wiki/Oyster_Bay_(town),_New_York "Oyster Bay (town), New York"); the [Old Westbury](/wiki/Old_Westbury,_New_York "Old Westbury, New York") campus of [New York Institute of Technology](/wiki/New_York_Institute_of_Technology "New York Institute of Technology"); the second campus of the [New York University Grossman School of Medicine](/wiki/New_York_University_Grossman_School_of_Medicine "New York University Grossman School of Medicine") in [Mineola](/wiki/Mineola,_New_York "Mineola, New York"), [Zucker School of Medicine](/wiki/Zucker_School_of_Medicine "Zucker School of Medicine") in the [Village of Hempstead](/wiki/Hempstead_(village),_New_York "Hempstead (village), New York"); and the [Feinstein Institutes for Medical Research](/wiki/Feinstein_Institutes_for_Medical_Research "Feinstein Institutes for Medical Research") in [Manhasset](/wiki/Manhasset,_New_York "Manhasset, New York"), are prominent [life sciences](/wiki/Biology "Biology") [research](/wiki/Research "Research") and academic institutions in Nassau County. The presence of numerous prominent [health care](/wiki/Health_care "Health care") systems has made Nassau County a central hub for advanced medical care and technology. Eight [cricket matches](/wiki/Cricket_(sport) "Cricket (sport)") of the [2024 ICC Men's T20 World Cup](/wiki/2024_ICC_Men%27s_T20_World_Cup "2024 ICC Men's T20 World Cup") were played at a temporary [cricket stadium](/wiki/Nassau_County_International_Cricket_Stadium "Nassau County International Cricket Stadium") in [Eisenhower Park](/wiki/Eisenhower_Park "Eisenhower Park") in [East Meadow](/wiki/East_Meadow,_New_York "East Meadow, New York") in June 2024.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nassau_County,_New_York&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name of Nassau County originated from an old name for [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), which was at one time named *Nassau*, after the [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") family of King [William III of England](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England"), the [House of Nassau](/wiki/House_of_Nassau "House of Nassau"),[[12]](#cite_note-12) itself named after the [German town of Nassau](/wiki/Nassau,_Rhineland-Palatinate "Nassau, Rhineland-Palatinate"). The county colors (orange and blue) are also the colors of the [House of Orange-Nassau](/wiki/House_of_Orange-Nassau "House of Orange-Nassau").

Several alternate names had been considered for the county, including "Bryant", "Matinecock" ([a village within the county](/wiki/Matinecock "Matinecock") currently has that name), "Norfolk" (presumably because of the proximity to Suffolk County), and "Sagamore".[[13]](#cite_note-13) However, "Nassau" had the historical advantage of having at one time been the name of Long Island itself,[[14]](#cite_note-14) and was the name most mentioned after the new county was proposed in 1875.[[15]](#cite_note-NYT1875-04-12-15)[[16]](#cite_note-NYT1876-04-09-16)[[17]](#cite_note-NYT1876-12-21-17)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nassau_County,_New_York&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The area now designated as Nassau County was originally the eastern 70% of [Queens County](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), one of the original twelve [counties](/wiki/County_(New_York) "County (New York)") formed in 1683, and was then contained within two towns: [Hempstead](/wiki/Hempstead_(town),_New_York "Hempstead (town), New York"), and [Oyster Bay](/wiki/Oyster_Bay_(town),_New_York "Oyster Bay (town), New York"). In 1784, the Town of North Hempstead, was formed through [secession](/wiki/Secession "Secession") by the northern portions of the Town of Hempstead. Nassau County was formed in 1899 by the division of Queens County, after the western portion of Queens had become a borough of New York City in 1898, as the three easternmost towns seceded from the county.

When the first [European](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Europe "Ethnic groups in Europe") settlers arrived, among the [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") to occupy the present area of Nassau County were the [Marsapeque, Matinecoc, and Sacatogue](/wiki/Metoac "Metoac"). [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people") settlers in [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland") predominated in the western portion of Long Island, while English settlers from Connecticut occupied the eastern portion. Until 1664, Long Island was split, roughly at the present border between Nassau and Suffolk counties, between the Dutch in the west and Connecticut claiming the east. The Dutch did grant an English settlement in Hempstead (now in western Nassau), but drove settlers from the present-day eastern Nassau [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(New_York) "Hamlet (New York)") of [Oyster Bay](/wiki/Oyster_Bay_(hamlet),_New_York "Oyster Bay (hamlet), New York") as part of a boundary dispute. In 1664, all of Long Island became part of the English [Province of New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York") within the [Shire of York](/wiki/York_Shire_(Province_of_New_York) "York Shire (Province of New York)"). Present-day Queens and Nassau were then just part of a larger North Riding. In 1683, the colonial territory of [Yorkshire](/wiki/York_Shire_(Province_of_New_York) "York Shire (Province of New York)") was dissolved, Suffolk County and Queens County were established, and the local seat of government was moved west from Hempstead to Jamaica (now in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")).[[18]](#cite_note-18)

By 1700, virtually none of Long Island's area remained unpurchased from the Native Americans by the English colonists, and townships controlled whatever land had not already been distributed.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The courthouse in Jamaica was torn down by the British during the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") to use the materials to build barracks.[[20]](#cite_note-HoQC-20)

In 1784, following the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), the Town of Hempstead was split in two, when [Patriots](/wiki/Patriot_(American_Revolution) "Patriot (American Revolution)") in the northern part formed the new [Town of North Hempstead](/wiki/North_Hempstead,_New_York "North Hempstead, New York"), leaving [Loyalist](/wiki/Loyalist_(American_Revolution) "Loyalist (American Revolution)") majorities in the Town of Hempstead. About 1787, a new Queens County Courthouse was erected (and later completed) in the new Town of North Hempstead, near present-day [Mineola](/wiki/Mineola,_New_York "Mineola, New York") (now in Nassau County), known then as Clowesville.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-NCPO-22)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-MineolaFarmers-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Mineola_LIRR_station_at_sunset.jpg/250px-Mineola_LIRR_station_at_sunset.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mineola_LIRR_station_at_sunset.jpg)

[Mineola Station](/wiki/Mineola_station_(LIRR) "Mineola station (LIRR)") of the [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road")

The [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") reached as far east as [Hicksville](/wiki/Hicksville,_New_York "Hicksville, New York") in 1837, but did not proceed to [Farmingdale](/wiki/Farmingdale,_New_York "Farmingdale, New York") until 1841 due to the [Panic of 1837](/wiki/Panic_of_1837 "Panic of 1837"). The 1850 census was the first in which the combined population of the three western towns (Flushing, Jamaica, and Newtown) exceeded that of the three eastern towns that are now part of Nassau County. Concerns were raised about the condition of the old courthouse and the inconvenience of travel and accommodations, with the three eastern and three western towns divided on the location for the construction of a new one.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Around 1874, the seat of county government was moved to [Long Island City](/wiki/Long_Island_City,_Queens "Long Island City, Queens") from Mineola.[[25]](#cite_note-MineolaFarmers-25)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-NewsdayNassau-28) As early as 1875, representatives of the three eastern towns began advocating the separation of the three eastern towns from Queens, with some proposals also including the towns of [Huntington](/wiki/Huntington,_New_York "Huntington, New York") and Babylon (in Suffolk County).[[15]](#cite_note-NYT1875-04-12-15)[[16]](#cite_note-NYT1876-04-09-16)[[17]](#cite_note-NYT1876-12-21-17)

In 1898, the western portion of Queens County became a borough of the [City of Greater New York](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York"), leaving the eastern portion a part of Queens County but not part of the Borough of Queens. As part of the city consolidation plan, all town, village, and city (other than NYC) governments within the borough were dissolved, as well as the county government with its seat in Jamaica. The areas excluded from the consolidation included all of the Town of North Hempstead, all of the Town of Oyster Bay, and most of the Town of Hempstead (excluding the [Rockaway Peninsula](/wiki/Rockaway,_Queens "Rockaway, Queens"), which was separated from the Town of Hempstead and became part of the city borough).

In 1899, following approval from the [New York State Legislature](/wiki/New_York_State_Legislature "New York State Legislature"), the three towns were separated from Queens County, and the new county of Nassau was constituted.

In preparation for the new county, in November 1898, voters had selected [Mineola](/wiki/Mineola,_New_York "Mineola, New York") to become the county seat for the new county[[29]](#cite_note-29) (before Mineola incorporated as a village in 1906 and set its boundaries almost entirely within the Town of North Hempstead), winning out over Hicksville and Hempstead.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The Garden City Company (founded in 1893 by the heirs of [Alexander Turney Stewart](/wiki/Alexander_Turney_Stewart "Alexander Turney Stewart"))[[31]](#cite_note-31) donated four acres of land for the county buildings in the Town of Hempstead, just south of the Mineola train station and the present day village of Mineola.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The land and the buildings have a Mineola postal address, but are within the present day [Village of Garden City](/wiki/Garden_City,_New_York "Garden City, New York"),[[33]](#cite_note-33) which did not incorporate, nor set its boundaries, until 1919.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Int495eRoad-Exit41S-NY106sNY107s_%2831061511095%29.jpg/250px-Int495eRoad-Exit41S-NY106sNY107s_%2831061511095%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Int495eRoad-Exit41S-NY106sNY107s_(31061511095).jpg)

[Long Island Expressway](/wiki/Long_Island_Expressway "Long Island Expressway") at [Hicksville](/wiki/Hicksville,_New_York "Hicksville, New York"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), home to a growing [Little India](/wiki/Little_India "Little India")

In 1917,[[34]](#cite_note-HOGC-34) the hamlet of [Glen Cove](/wiki/Glen_Cove,_New_York "Glen Cove, New York") was granted a city charter, making it independent from the Town of Oyster Bay. In 1918, the village of [Long Beach](/wiki/Long_Beach,_New_York "Long Beach, New York") was incorporated in the Town of Hempstead. In 1922, it became a city, making it independent of the town. These are the only two [administrative divisions](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York#City "Administrative divisions of New York") in Nassau County identified as cities.

From the early 1900s until the Depression and the early 1930s, many hilly farmlands on the North Shore were transformed into luxurious country estates for wealthy New Yorkers, with the area receiving the ["Gold Coast"](/wiki/North_Shore_(Long_Island)#Gold_Coast "North Shore (Long Island)") moniker and becoming the setting of [F. Scott Fitzgerald](/wiki/F._Scott_Fitzgerald "F. Scott Fitzgerald")'s 1925 novel *[The Great Gatsby](/wiki/The_Great_Gatsby "The Great Gatsby")*. One summer resident of the Gold Coast was President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"), at [Sagamore Hill](/wiki/Sagamore_Hill "Sagamore Hill"). In 1908, [William Kissam Vanderbilt](/wiki/William_Kissam_Vanderbilt "William Kissam Vanderbilt") constructed the [Long Island Motor Parkway](/wiki/Long_Island_Motor_Parkway "Long Island Motor Parkway") as a [toll road](/wiki/Toll_road "Toll road") through Nassau County. With overpasses and bridges to remove intersections, it was among the first [limited access motor highways](/wiki/Freeway "Freeway") in the world, and was also used as a racecourse to test the capabilities of the fledgling automobile industry.

Nassau County, with its extensive flat land, was the site of many [aviation](/wiki/Aviation "Aviation") firsts.[[35]](#cite_note-CoA-35) Military aviators for both World Wars were trained on the [Hempstead Plains](/wiki/Hempstead_Plains "Hempstead Plains") at installations such as [Mitchel Air Force Base](/wiki/Mitchel_Air_Force_Base "Mitchel Air Force Base"), and a number of successful aircraft companies were established. [Charles Lindbergh](/wiki/Charles_Lindbergh "Charles Lindbergh") took off for Paris from [Roosevelt Field](/wiki/Roosevelt_Field_(airport) "Roosevelt Field (airport)") in 1927, completing the first non-stop trans-Atlantic flight from the United States. [Grumman](/wiki/Grumman "Grumman") (which in 1986 employed 23,000 people on Long Island[[36]](#cite_note-36)) built many planes for [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), and later contributed the [Apollo Lunar Module](/wiki/Apollo_Lunar_Module "Apollo Lunar Module") to the Space program.[[35]](#cite_note-CoA-35)

The [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") was temporarily located in Nassau County, from 1946 till 1951. Council meetings were held at the [Sperry](/wiki/Sperry_Corporation "Sperry Corporation") Gyroscope headquarters in the village of [Lake Success](/wiki/Lake_Success,_New_York "Lake Success, New York"), near the border with Queens County. It was here that on June 27, 1950, the Security Council voted to back U.S. President [Harry S Truman](/wiki/Harry_S_Truman "Harry S Truman") and send a [coalition](/wiki/Coalition "Coalition") of forces to the [Korean Peninsula](/wiki/Korean_Peninsula "Korean Peninsula"), leading to the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War").

Until World War II, most of Nassau County was still farmland, particularly in the eastern portion. Following the war, the county saw an influx of people from the [five boroughs of New York City](/wiki/Borough_(New_York_City) "Borough (New York City)"), especially from Brooklyn and Queens, who left their urban dwellings for a more suburban setting. This led to a massive population boom in the county. In 1947, [William Levitt](/wiki/William_Levitt "William Levitt") built his first [planned community](/wiki/Planned_community "Planned community") in Nassau County, in the Island Trees section (later renamed [Levittown](/wiki/Levittown,_New_York "Levittown, New York"); this should not be confused with the county's first planned community, which in general is [Garden City](/wiki/Garden_City,_New_York "Garden City, New York")). In the 1930s, [Robert Moses](/wiki/Robert_Moses "Robert Moses") had engineered curving [parkways](/wiki/Parkways "Parkways") and parks such as [Jones Beach State Park](/wiki/Jones_Beach_State_Park "Jones Beach State Park") and [Bethpage State Park](/wiki/Bethpage_State_Park "Bethpage State Park") for the enjoyment of city-dwellers; in the 1950s and 1960s the focus turned to alleviating commuter traffic.

In 1994, Federal Judge Arthur Spatt declared the Nassau County Board of Supervisors unconstitutional and directed that a 19-member legislature be formed.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Republicans won 13 seats in the election and chose [Bruce Blakeman](/wiki/Bruce_Blakeman "Bruce Blakeman") as the first Presiding Officer (Speaker).[[38]](#cite_note-38)

According to a *Forbes* magazine 2012 survey, residents of Nassau County have the 12th highest median household annual income in the country and the highest in the state.[[9]](#cite_note-Forbes-2012-ranks-Nassau-12th-9) In the 1990s, however, Nassau County experienced substantial budget problems, forcing the county to near [bankruptcy](/wiki/Bankruptcy "Bankruptcy"). Thus, the county government increased taxes to prevent a takeover by the state of New York, leading to the county having high [property taxes](/wiki/Property_tax "Property tax"). Nevertheless, on January 27, 2011, a State of New York oversight board seized control of Nassau County's finances, saying the wealthy and heavily taxed county had failed to balance its $2.6 billion budgets.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

In 2024, Blakeman, in his role as county executive, began recruiting for a new armed force separate from the county's police force, saying the new group was intended for activation in case of emergencies.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The plan drew major controversy after Blakeman stated that such emergency deployments could include riot control in the context of political protests.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Blakeman, a [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") ally recently nominated as the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") candidate in the [2026 New York gubernatorial election](/wiki/2026_New_York_gubernatorial_election "2026 New York gubernatorial election"), was accused by opponents of forming a personal militia. In March 2026, a lawsuit was filed on behalf of county legislators affiliated with the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), calling for the force to be disbanded, based partly on evidence that some recruits were not qualified to carry out police work.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Hempstead%2C_Manhasset%2C_and_Little_Neck_bays.jpg/250px-Hempstead%2C_Manhasset%2C_and_Little_Neck_bays.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hempstead,_Manhasset,_and_Little_Neck_bays.jpg)

The [Cow Neck Peninsula](/wiki/Cow_Neck_Peninsula "Cow Neck Peninsula") is visible as the first peninsula at the center, with [Manhasset Bay](/wiki/Manhasset_Bay "Manhasset Bay") immediately above it and [Hempstead Harbor](/wiki/Hempstead_Harbor "Hempstead Harbor") immediately below it.

According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau"), the county has a total area of 453.2 square miles (1,174 km2), of which 284.7 square miles (737 km2) is land and 168.5 square miles (436 km2) (37%) is water.[[43]](#cite_note-GR1-43)

Nassau County borders the [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound") on the north and the open [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") on the south. The highest point in the county is [Harbor Hill](/wiki/Harbor_Hill "Harbor Hill") on the north shore. The county occupies a portion of Long Island immediately east of the New York City borough of Queens. It is [divided into](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York "Administrative divisions of New York") two cities and three towns, the latter of which contain 64 villages and numerous hamlets. The county borders [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") across the Long Island Sound.

Between the [1990 U.S. census](/wiki/1990_United_States_census "1990 United States census") and the [2000 U.S. census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"), the Nassau County exchanged territory with Suffolk County and lost territory to Queens County.[[44]](#cite_note-PopH2000-44) Dozens of CDPs had boundaries changed, and 12 new CDPs were listed.[[44]](#cite_note-PopH2000-44)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nassau_County,_New_York&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

Nassau County has a climate similar to other coastal areas of the [Northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"); it has warm, humid summers and cool, wet winters. The county's climate is classified as [humid subtropical](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa*) according to the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"). According to the [Trewartha climate classification](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification "Trewartha climate classification") the climate is oceanic (*Do*) since six to seven months average above 50″F (10″C). The Atlantic Ocean helps bring afternoon sea breezes that temper the heat in the warmer months and limit the frequency and severity of thunderstorms. Nassau County has a moderately sunny climate, averaging between 2,400 and 2,800 hours of [sunshine](/wiki/Sunshine "Sunshine") annually.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The [hardiness zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") is 7b.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nassau_County,_New_York&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Transportation")]

In July 2017, the approval was granted by state legislators to the plan proposed by [New York Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_York "Governor of New York") [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo") to add a third railroad track to the [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") corridor between the communities of [Floral Park](/wiki/Floral_Park_(LIRR_station) "Floral Park (LIRR station)") and [Hicksville](/wiki/Hicksville,_New_York "Hicksville, New York") in Nassau County. The nearly US$2 billion [transportation infrastructure](/wiki/Transportation_infrastructure "Transportation infrastructure") enhancement project was expected to accommodate anticipated growth in rail ridership and facilitate commutes between New York City and Nassau and Suffolk counties on Long Island.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

The [Long Island Expressway](/wiki/Long_Island_Expressway "Long Island Expressway"), [Northern State Parkway](/wiki/Northern_State_Parkway "Northern State Parkway"), and [Southern State Parkway](/wiki/Southern_State_Parkway "Southern State Parkway") are the primary east–west [controlled-access highways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") in Nassau County. [Northern Boulevard](/wiki/Northern_Boulevard "Northern Boulevard") (New York State Route 25A), [Hillside Avenue](/wiki/Hillside_Avenue "Hillside Avenue") (New York State Route 25B), [Jericho Turnpike](/wiki/Jericho_Turnpike "Jericho Turnpike") (New York State Route 25), [New York State Route 24](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_24 "New York State Route 24"), and [Sunrise Highway](/wiki/Sunrise_Highway "Sunrise Highway") (New York State Route 27) are also major east–west commercial [thoroughfares](/wiki/Thoroughfare "Thoroughfare") across the county. The [Meadowbrook State Parkway](/wiki/Meadowbrook_State_Parkway "Meadowbrook State Parkway"), [Wantagh State Parkway](/wiki/Wantagh_State_Parkway "Wantagh State Parkway"), and [Seaford–Oyster Bay Expressway](/wiki/Seaford%E2%80%93Oyster_Bay_Expressway "Seaford–Oyster Bay Expressway") (New York State Route 135) are the major north–south controlled-access highways traversing Nassau County.

Nassau County also has a public bus network known as [NICE](/wiki/Nassau_Inter-County_Express "Nassau Inter-County Express") (Nassau Inter-County Express, formerly MTA Long Island Bus) that operates routes throughout the county into Queens and Suffolk counties. 24 hour service is provided on the n4, n6, and most recently the n40/41 lines.

At the 2019 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey"), the population of Nassau County stood at 1,356,924, an increase of 17,392 since the 2010 census.[[56]](#cite_note-56) At the [2010 U.S. census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"), there were 1,339,532 people, 448,528 households, and 340,523 families residing in the county. The population of Nassau County was estimated by the U.S. Census Bureau to have increased by 2.2% to 1,369,514 in 2017, representing 6.9% of the census-estimated State of New York population of 19,849,399[[57]](#cite_note-NYSQuickFacts-57) and 17.4% of the census-estimated Long Island population of 7,869,820.[[58]](#cite_note-Kings_County,_New_York_QuickFacts-58)[[59]](#cite_note-Queens_County,_New_York_QuickFacts-59)[[60]](#cite_note-Nassau_County,_New_York_QuickFacts-60)[[61]](#cite_note-U.S._Census_Bureau-61) At the [2000 United States census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"), there were 1,334,544 people, 447,387 households, and 347,172 families residing in the county.

In 2010, there were 340,523 family households. 33.5% had children under the age of 18 living with them. 60.0% were married couples living together. 11.7% had a female householder with no husband present. 24.1% were non-families. 20.1% of all households were made up of individuals. 15.1% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.94. The average family size was 3.38.[[62]](#cite_note-2010Nassau-62)

In 2010, the population was 23.3% under the age of 18. 18.7% were 62 years of age or older. The median age was 41.1 years. For every 100 females, there were 93.7 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 90.4 males.[[62]](#cite_note-2010Nassau-62) In 2019, there were 474,165 housing units and 446,977 family households.[[63]](#cite_note-:0-63) From 2015 to 2019, there was an average of 2.99 persons per household, and 21.4% of the population was under 18 years of age.

At the 2019 American Community Survey, Nassau had a median household income of $116,100. The per capita income was $51,422. About 5.6% of the population lived at or below the poverty line.[[63]](#cite_note-:0-63) The median income for a household in the county in 2010 was $72,030. and the median income for a family was $81,246. These figures had risen to $87,658 and $101,661 respectively according to a 2007 estimate.[[64]](#cite_note-64) Males had a median income of $52,340 versus $37,446 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the county was $32,151. About 3.50% of families and 5.20% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 5.80% of those under age 18 and 5.60% of those age 65 or over.

The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") in 2010 was 4,700 people per square mile (1,800 people/km2). In 2000, the population density was 4,655 inhabitants per square mile (1,797/km2). In the 2010 census, there were 468,346 housing units at an average density of 1,598 per square mile (617/km2).

In 2011, there were about 230,000 [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish") people in Nassau County,[[76]](#cite_note-76) representing 17.2% of the population, (as compared to 2% of the total U.S. population). [Italian Americans](/wiki/Italians_in_New_York_City "Italians in New York City") also made up a large portion of Nassau's population. The five most reported ancestries were Italian (23%), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American") (14%), [German](/wiki/German_American "German American") (7%), [Indian](/wiki/Indian_Americans "Indian Americans") (5%), and [Polish](/wiki/Polish_American "Polish American") (4%). The county's population was highest at the [1970 U.S. census](/wiki/1970_United_States_census "1970 United States census"). More recently, a [Little India](/wiki/Little_India_(location) "Little India (location)") community has emerged in [Hicksville](/wiki/Hicksville,_New_York "Hicksville, New York"), Nassau County,[[77]](#cite_note-77) spreading eastward from the more established Little India enclaves in Queens. Rapidly growing [Chinatowns](/wiki/Chinatowns_in_New_York "Chinatowns in New York") have developed in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Chinatowns_in_Brooklyn "Chinatowns in Brooklyn") and [Queens](/wiki/Chinatowns_in_Queens "Chinatowns in Queens"),[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-LongIslandAsians-80) as did earlier European immigrants, such as the Irish and Italians.

As of 2019, the Asian population in Nassau County had grown by 39% since 2010, to an estimated 145,191 individuals. There were approximately 50,000 [Indian Americans](/wiki/Indians_in_the_New_York_City_metropolitan_region "Indians in the New York City metropolitan region") and 40,000 [Chinese Americans](/wiki/Chinese_Americans_in_New_York_City "Chinese Americans in New York City"). Nassau County has become the leading [suburban](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") destination in the U.S. for [Chinese immigrants](/wiki/Chinese_emigration "Chinese emigration").[[81]](#cite_note-81) Likewise, the [Long Island Koreatown](/wiki/Koreatown,_Long_Island "Koreatown, Long Island") originated in [Flushing](/wiki/Flushing,_Queens "Flushing, Queens"), Queens, and is expanding eastward along [Northern Boulevard](/wiki/Northern_Boulevard "Northern Boulevard")[[82]](#cite_note-KT1-82)[[83]](#cite_note-Meokjagolmok-83)[[84]](#cite_note-KT2-84)[[85]](#cite_note-KT3-85)[[86]](#cite_note-KT4-86) and into Nassau County.[[80]](#cite_note-LongIslandAsians-80)[[83]](#cite_note-Meokjagolmok-83)[[84]](#cite_note-KT2-84) *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* cited a 2002 study by the non-profit group ERASE Racism, which determined that Nassau, and its neighboring county, Suffolk, as the most [de facto racially segregated](/wiki/Racial_segregation_in_the_United_States "Racial segregation in the United States") suburbs in the United States.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

## Law enforcement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nassau_County,_New_York&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Law enforcement")]

Main article: [Nassau County Police Department](/wiki/Nassau_County_Police_Department "Nassau County Police Department")

[County police](/wiki/County_police "County police") services are provided by the [Nassau County Police Department](/wiki/Nassau_County_Police_Department "Nassau County Police Department"). The cities of [Glen Cove](/wiki/Glen_Cove,_New_York "Glen Cove, New York") and [Long Beach](/wiki/Long_Beach,_New_York "Long Beach, New York"), as well as a number of villages, are not members of the county police district and maintain their own police forces. The following village police departments exist in Nassau County: Brookville (Brookville P.D. provides police protection for Brookville, Matinecock, Mill Neck and Cove Neck), Centre Island, Floral Park, Freeport, Garden City, Great Neck Estates, Hempstead, Kensington, Kings Point, Lake Success, Lynbrook, Malverne, Muttontown-Upper Brookville, Old Brookville, Old Westbury, Oyster Bay Cove, Rockville Centre and Sands Point.

The [Port Washington Police District](/wiki/Port_Washington_Police_District "Port Washington Police District") is not a village department but is authorized by a special district, the only such district in the State of New York. These smaller forces make use of such specialized county police services as the police academy and the aviation unit. All homicides in the county are investigated by the county police, regardless of whether or not they occur within the police district.

In June 2011, the Muttontown Police Department commenced operations. The Old Brookville Police had formerly provided police services to the Village of Muttontown.

On June 1, 2022, the Old Brookville Police Department reverted to serving only the Village of Old Brookville and moved its headquarters to the grounds of the Old Brookville village hall. The Village of Brookville formed a new police department, established headquarters on the grounds of the Brookville Nature Park and assumed policing duties for the villages of Brookville, Matinecock, Mill Neck and Cove Neck, that were formerly served by the Old Brookville Police Department. The Village of Upper Brookville joined the Muttontown Police Department which was subsequently renamed the Muttontown-Upper Brookville (MUB) Police Department. The former Old Brookville Police headquarters is now the Upper Brookville village hall and also a substation for the Muttontown-Upper Brookville Police Department.

In 2006, village leaders in the county seat of [Mineola](/wiki/Mineola,_New_York "Mineola, New York") expressed dissatisfaction with the level of police coverage provided by the county force and actively explored seceding from the police district and having the village form its own police force. A referendum in December 2006 decisively defeated the proposal.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

Since the [Long Island State Parkway Police](/wiki/Long_Island_State_Parkway_Police "Long Island State Parkway Police") was disbanded in 1980, all of Nassau County's state parkways have been patrolled by Troop L of the [New York State Police](/wiki/New_York_State_Police "New York State Police"). State parks in Nassau are patrolled by the [New York State Park Police](/wiki/New_York_State_Park_Police "New York State Park Police"). In 1996, the [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") Police Department was consolidated into the [Metropolitan Transportation Authority Police](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority_Police "Metropolitan Transportation Authority Police"). The MTA Police patrol Long Island Rail Road tracks, stations and properties. The [New York State Department of Environmental Conservation Police](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Environmental_Conservation_Police "New York State Department of Environmental Conservation Police") provides enforcement of state environmental laws and regulations. The [State University of New York Police](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York_Police "State University of New York Police") provides enforcement for [SUNY Old Westbury](/wiki/SUNY_Old_Westbury "SUNY Old Westbury").

The Nassau County Police Department posts the mug shots of [DWI](/wiki/Driving_While_Intoxicated "Driving While Intoxicated") offenders as press releases on their website. This practice has come under the scrutiny of residents, media, and those pictured in these press releases. This practice has been criticized as being able to cost potential employees, students, or public figures their positions.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

County correctional services and enforcement of court orders are provided by the [Nassau County Sheriff's Department](/wiki/Nassau_County_Sheriff%27s_Department_(New_York) "Nassau County Sheriff's Department (New York)"). [New York State Court Officers](/wiki/New_York_State_Court_Officers "New York State Court Officers") provide security for [courthouses](/wiki/Courthouse "Courthouse").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/NCAP_RMP.jpg/250px-NCAP_RMP.jpg)](/wiki/File:NCAP_RMP.jpg)

Nassau County Auxiliary Police car

The Nassau County Auxiliary Police are a unit of the [Nassau County Police Department](/wiki/Nassau_County_Police_Department "Nassau County Police Department"). These volunteer police officers are assigned to 1 of 38 local [community units](/wiki/Community_policing "Community policing") and perform routine patrols of the neighborhood. They provide traffic control for local parades, races and other community events. Auxiliary Police officers are empowered to make arrests for crimes that occur in their presence.

Nassau County Auxiliary Police are required to complete a 42-week training course at the Nassau County Police Academy. Qualified officers are offered Emergency Medical Technician (EMT) training. Auxiliary Police officers are certified and registered by the New York Division of Criminal Justice Services as full-time "peace officers". The [City of Long Beach](/wiki/Long_Beach,_New_York "Long Beach, New York") has an independent [auxiliary police force](/wiki/Long_Beach_Police_Department_(New_York) "Long Beach Police Department (New York)") which is part of its municipal police force. These officers are represented by the Auxiliary Police Benevolent Association of Long Island.

The head of the county's governmental structure is the county executive, a post created in Nassau County in 1938. The current county executive is [Bruce Blakeman](/wiki/Bruce_Blakeman "Bruce Blakeman"), a [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") who was elected in 2021. The chief deputy county executive is Republican Arthur Walsh. The district attorney is [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") Anne T. Donnelly, who was elected in 2021, replacing Acting District Attorney Joyce Smith. Smith succeeded [Madeline Singas](/wiki/Madeline_Singas "Madeline Singas") after she was nominated and confirmed as an associate judge on the [New York Court of Appeals](/wiki/New_York_Court_of_Appeals "New York Court of Appeals") in June 2021.

The county [comptroller](/wiki/Comptroller "Comptroller") is [Elaine Phillips](/wiki/Elaine_Phillips "Elaine Phillips"), a Republican who formerly served in the [New York State Senate](/wiki/New_York_State_Senate "New York State Senate"). The [county clerk](/wiki/County_clerk "County clerk") is Republican [Maureen O'Connell](/wiki/Maureen_O%27Connell "Maureen O'Connell"). Former elected offices chairman of the County Board of Assessors, county treasurer, and county sheriff were made appointed and serve at the pleasure of the county executive (county assessor in 2008 via referendum, changing it from a six-year term to appointed).[[93]](#cite_note-93)

### Chief deputy county executive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nassau_County,_New_York&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Chief deputy county executive")]

The chief deputy county executive[[94]](#cite_note-94) is the highest appointed official in the Nassau County government, serving second-in-command under the auspice of the county executive. The Chief Deputy is responsible for managing the activities of all departments of the Nassau County government, which provides services to its 1.36 million residents. The chief deputy also officially serves as the acting county executive in the absence of, or disability of the County Executive. The current chief deputy county executive is Arthur T. For most of the twentieth century, residents of Nassau County and neighboring [Suffolk County](/wiki/Suffolk_County,_New_York "Suffolk County, New York") primarily supported the Republican Party in national elections. In presidential elections during the first half of the century, the Republican candidate often received more than twice as many votes as the Democratic candidate. Between the county's incorporation in 1899 and the 1980s, Democrats only won Nassau County in the elections of [1912](/wiki/1912_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1912 United States presidential election in New York") (where [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt")'s [Progressive Party](/wiki/Progressive_Party_(United_States,_1912%E2%80%931920) "Progressive Party (United States, 1912–1920)") split the Republican vote) and [1964](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1964 United States presidential election in New York") (where [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") won in a landslide).

The county began trending Democratic in the 1990s, like many of New York City's suburbs. From 1992 to 2020, it voted for a Democrat in every presidential election. [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") carried the county in [1992](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1992 United States presidential election in New York") and [1996](/wiki/1996_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1996 United States presidential election in New York"), as did [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore") in [2000](/wiki/2000_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2000 United States presidential election in New York"), the latter two times by margins of nearly 20 points. [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry")'s margin in Nassau County was considerably slimmer (5.6 points) in [2004](/wiki/2004_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2004 United States presidential election in New York"), as he won the towns of Hempstead and North Hempstead but lost the town of Oyster Bay. The county went solidly for [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") in [2008](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2008 United States presidential election in New York") and [2012](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2012 United States presidential election in New York"), both times by around 8 points. [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") did marginally worse in [2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2016 United States presidential election in New York"), winning by 6 points. [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") in [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2020 United States presidential election in New York") fared better than Obama to win the county by 9.5 percentage points, but still not as well as Bill Clinton and Gore.

The streak Democratic candidates carrying the county ended in [2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2024 United States presidential election in New York"), as [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") carried the county by over four percentage points, the first time Nassau was won by a Republican presidential candidate since [1988](/wiki/1988_United_States_presidential_election "1988 United States presidential election").[[96]](#cite_note-96)

Democratic strength is chiefly concentrated in both the wealthier and lower income sections of the county. Liberal voters dominate many of the wealthy communities of the North Shore, particularly in the [Town of North Hempstead](/wiki/North_Hempstead,_New_York "North Hempstead, New York") where affluent villages such as [Sands Point](/wiki/Sands_Point,_New_York "Sands Point, New York"), [Old Westbury](/wiki/Old_Westbury,_New_York "Old Westbury, New York"), [Roslyn](/wiki/Roslyn,_New_York "Roslyn, New York"), [Kensington](/wiki/Kensington,_New_York "Kensington, New York"), [Thomaston](/wiki/Thomaston,_New_York "Thomaston, New York"), [Great Neck Plaza](/wiki/Great_Neck_Plaza,_New_York "Great Neck Plaza, New York"), and [Great Neck Estates](/wiki/Great_Neck_Estates,_New_York "Great Neck Estates, New York") as well as the neighboring City of [Glen Cove](/wiki/Glen_Cove,_New_York "Glen Cove, New York") vote consistently Democratic. Democratic strongholds also include several low income municipalities in the central portion of the county, such as the [Village of Hempstead](/wiki/Hempstead_(village),_New_York "Hempstead (village), New York"), [Roosevelt](/wiki/Roosevelt,_New_York "Roosevelt, New York"), [Uniondale](/wiki/Uniondale,_New_York "Uniondale, New York") and [New Cassel](/wiki/New_Cassel,_New_York "New Cassel, New York"), as well as in a few waterfront communities on the South Shore, such as the City of [Long Beach](/wiki/Long_Beach,_New_York "Long Beach, New York") and the Village of [Freeport](/wiki/Freeport,_New_York "Freeport, New York").

Republican voters are primarily concentrated in the middle to upper middle class southeastern portion of the county, which developed during the "post-war boom era". Heavily Republican communities such as [Massapequa](/wiki/Massapequa,_New_York "Massapequa, New York"), [Massapequa Park](/wiki/Massapequa_Park,_New_York "Massapequa Park, New York"), [Seaford](/wiki/Seaford,_New_York "Seaford, New York"), [Wantagh](/wiki/Wantagh,_New_York "Wantagh, New York"), [Levittown](/wiki/Levittown,_New_York "Levittown, New York"), [Bethpage](/wiki/Bethpage,_New_York "Bethpage, New York"), and [Farmingdale](/wiki/Farmingdale,_New_York "Farmingdale, New York") are the political base of many county GOP officials such as former Congressman [Peter T. King](/wiki/Peter_T._King "Peter T. King") and former County Executive [Edward P. Mangano](/wiki/Edward_P._Mangano "Edward P. Mangano"). In the western portion of the county, wealthy [Garden City](/wiki/Garden_City,_New_York "Garden City, New York") is solidly Republican, as is the middle-class community of [Floral Park](/wiki/Floral_Park,_New_York "Floral Park, New York"). Additionally, some of the more rustic areas of the North Shore, particularly in the Town of [Oyster Bay](/wiki/Oyster_Bay,_New_York "Oyster Bay, New York") usually vote for the GOP.

Areas of the county containing large numbers of swing voters include [East Meadow](/wiki/East_Meadow,_New_York "East Meadow, New York"), [Oceanside](/wiki/Oceanside,_New_York "Oceanside, New York"), and [Rockville Centre](/wiki/Rockville_Centre,_New_York "Rockville Centre, New York") on the South Shore and [Mineola](/wiki/Mineola,_New_York "Mineola, New York") on the North Shore. Several areas have changed in partisan affiliation. Formerly Democratic strongholds such as the [Five Towns](/wiki/Five_Towns "Five Towns") and parts of [Great Neck](/wiki/Great_Neck,_New_York "Great Neck, New York") have trended to the GOP while previously Republican areas such as [Elmont](/wiki/Elmont,_New_York "Elmont, New York"), [Valley Stream](/wiki/Valley_Stream,_New_York "Valley Stream, New York") and [Baldwin](/wiki/Baldwin,_Nassau_County,_New_York "Baldwin, Nassau County, New York") have become Democratic bastions.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nassau_County,_New_York&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Education")]

Education features strongly in Nassau County's culture.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

Nassau County has 58 public [school districts](/wiki/School_district "School district"),[[98]](#cite_note-Districtmapandlist-98) which like post office districts use the same names as a city, hamlet, or village within them, but each sets the boundaries independently.[[99]](#cite_note-99) School district and community are not the same, and residences often have postal addresses that differ from the hamlet and/or school district in which they are located. Several of Nassau County's school districts are among the highest ranked public school systems in the country,[[100]](#cite_note-100) including the [Jericho Union Free School District](/wiki/Jericho_Union_Free_School_District "Jericho Union Free School District"), [Great Neck Public Schools](/wiki/Great_Neck_Public_Schools "Great Neck Public Schools"), and the [Syosset Central School District](/wiki/Syosset_Central_School_District "Syosset Central School District").

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nassau_County,_New_York&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Bethpage_Golf_Course.jpg/250px-Bethpage_Golf_Course.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bethpage_Golf_Course.jpg)

Golf course at [Bethpage State Park](/wiki/Bethpage_State_Park "Bethpage State Park")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Belmont8_1999-05.jpg/250px-Belmont8_1999-05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belmont8_1999-05.jpg)

Horse racing at [Belmont Park](/wiki/Belmont_Park "Belmont Park")

Nassau County is home to the [New York Islanders](/wiki/New_York_Islanders "New York Islanders") of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"), who played at the [Nassau Veterans Memorial Coliseum](/wiki/Nassau_Veterans_Memorial_Coliseum "Nassau Veterans Memorial Coliseum") in [Uniondale](/wiki/Uniondale,_New_York "Uniondale, New York") from their inception in 1972. However, the Islanders announced in 2012 that starting in the fall of 2015, the team would be moving to [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") and would play at the [Barclays Center](/wiki/Barclays_Center "Barclays Center"). Due to issues with Barclays Center being unable to adequately support ice hockey and declining attendance, the Islanders announced that for the [2018–19 season](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_NHL_season "2018–19 NHL season") they would split their home games between Barclays Center and the newly renovated [Nassau Coliseum](/wiki/Nassau_Coliseum "Nassau Coliseum"). In December 2017, the Islanders won a bid to build a new 18,000-seat arena near [Belmont Park](/wiki/Belmont_Park "Belmont Park") in [Elmont](/wiki/Elmont,_New_York "Elmont, New York"), returning them to Nassau County; [UBS Arena](/wiki/UBS_Arena "UBS Arena") opened in 2021.

The [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets") of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"), then known as the New York Nets, formerly played their home games in Nassau County at the now-demolished [Island Garden](/wiki/Island_Garden "Island Garden") arena in [West Hempstead](/wiki/West_Hempstead "West Hempstead") from 1969 to 1972 and then at the Coliseum from 1972 to 1977, before the franchise moved to New Jersey—its original home for several years before coming to Long Island in the late 1960s – and eventually, to Brooklyn.

The [New York Cosmos (1970–1985)](/wiki/New_York_Cosmos_(1971%E2%80%931985) "New York Cosmos (1971–1985)") of the former [North American Soccer League (1968–1984)](/wiki/North_American_Soccer_League_(1968%E2%80%931984) "North American Soccer League (1968–1984)") played for two seasons, 1972 and 1973, at [Hofstra Stadium](/wiki/James_M._Shuart_Stadium "James M. Shuart Stadium") at Hofstra University in [Hempstead](/wiki/Hempstead,_New_York "Hempstead, New York"). The team's name was revived in 2010 with the [New York Cosmos (2010)](/wiki/New_York_Cosmos_(2013%E2%80%932020) "New York Cosmos (2013–2020)") of the new [North American Soccer League](/wiki/North_American_Soccer_League_(2011%E2%80%932017) "North American Soccer League (2011–2017)") to also play at Hofstra Stadium, which had been renamed [James M. Shuart Stadium](/wiki/James_M._Shuart_Stadium "James M. Shuart Stadium") in 2002. Nassau County is also the home of the [New York Lizards](/wiki/New_York_Lizards "New York Lizards") of [Major League Lacrosse](/wiki/Major_League_Lacrosse "Major League Lacrosse"), who play at Shuart Stadium. The county also operates several sports events for student-athletes, such as the Nassau County Executive Cup College Showcase.

[Belmont Park](/wiki/Belmont_Park "Belmont Park") in [Elmont](/wiki/Elmont,_New_York "Elmont, New York") is a major [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") venue which annually hosts the [Belmont Stakes](/wiki/Belmont_Stakes "Belmont Stakes"), the third and final leg of the prestigious [Triple Crown of thoroughbred racing](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing_(United_States) "Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing (United States)"). The now-demolished [Roosevelt Raceway](/wiki/Roosevelt_Raceway "Roosevelt Raceway") in [Westbury](/wiki/Westbury,_New_York "Westbury, New York") hosted auto racing and, from 1940 through 1988, was a popular [harness racing](/wiki/Harness_racing "Harness racing") track.

Nassau is home to some famous and historic [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf") courses. [Rockaway Hunting Club](/wiki/Rockaway_Hunting_Club "Rockaway Hunting Club"), founded in 1878, is the oldest country club in the country.[[101]](#cite_note-Our_Story-101) The [U.S. Open](/wiki/U.S._Open_(golf) "U.S. Open (golf)") has been held in Nassau five times, once each at [Garden City Golf Club](/wiki/Garden_City_Golf_Club "Garden City Golf Club"), [Inwood Country Club](/wiki/Inwood_Country_Club "Inwood Country Club"), and [Fresh Meadow Country Club](/wiki/Fresh_Meadow_Country_Club "Fresh Meadow Country Club"), and twice at [Bethpage Black Course](/wiki/Bethpage_Black_Course "Bethpage Black Course"), the first ever municipally owned course. Courses consistently ranked in the top 100 in the U.S. such as Bethpage Black, Garden City Golf Club, [Piping Rock Club](/wiki/Piping_Rock_Club "Piping Rock Club"), and The Creek are located in the county. Nassau County hosted the [1984 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Paralympics "1984 Summer Paralympics"), marking the first [Paralympic Games](/wiki/Paralympic_Games "Paralympic Games") to be held in the United States.

Nassau County hosted eight [cricket matches](/wiki/Cricket_(sport) "Cricket (sport)") of the [2024 ICC Men's T20 World Cup](/wiki/2024_ICC_Men%27s_T20_World_Cup "2024 ICC Men's T20 World Cup") at Eisenhower Park in [East Meadow](/wiki/East_Meadow,_New_York "East Meadow, New York") during June 2024.[[102]](#cite_note-NassauCountyPermanentCricketImprovements1-102)[[103]](#cite_note-NassauCountyPermanentCricketImprovements2-103)

## Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nassau_County,_New_York&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Health")]

The first case of [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States") was reported in March 2020.[[104]](#cite_note-104) As of January 12, 2021, there have been 104,078 cases, 3,044 deaths, 2,102,900 tests conducted, and a 4.9% positivity rate.[[105]](#cite_note-105) According to *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")'* COVID-19 tracker, Nassau County's average daily case count is 1,567 (116 per capita), with 1 in 13 testing positive (the third-worst of any county in the state) and 1 in 545 dying.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

In August 2024, Nassau County passed into law a ban on wearing face masks in public, making it a misdemeanor subject to a $1,000 fine and up to one year in prison to wear a facial covering in public, a move that was criticized by the [New York Civil Liberties Union](/wiki/New_York_Civil_Liberties_Union "New York Civil Liberties Union") as a "dangerous misuse of the law to score political points."[[107]](#cite_note-:1-107) The law does not apply to facial coverings "worn to protect the health or safety of the wearer," but does appear to ban wearing a mask in order to protect the health or safety of others, including persons with [compromised immune systems](/wiki/Immunodeficiency "Immunodeficiency").[[107]](#cite_note-:1-107)
